title.en: A skill a teammate has now that they didn't when you started
title.es: Una habilidad que un compañero tiene ahora que no tenía cuando empezaron

question.en: Tell me about a specific skill or capability a teammate has now that they didn't have when you started working with them — and what you did to help them get there.
question.es: Cuéntame sobre una habilidad o capacidad específica que un compañero tiene ahora que no tenía cuando comenzaron a trabajar juntos — y qué hiciste para ayudarlos a llegar ahí.

must_include: the specific teammate named by name — 'a junior engineer' or 'someone on my team' is not sufficient; if this is real, the candidate remembers the name
the specific skill or capability that changed — 'grew as an engineer' is not a skill; 'now independently designs and reviews distributed systems failure modes' is

the specific thing the candidate did — not 'I mentored them' but the concrete mechanism: giving ownership of a module, reviewing with detailed failure-mode comments, co-authoring and then stepping back, running deliberate scenario exercises
a concrete artifact that demonstrates the capability now exists — a PR they wrote independently, a design doc they authored, a presentation they led, an incident post-mortem they drove

evidence the capability belongs to the teammate, not the candidate — 'they told me they felt confident for the first time' or 'they were assigned lead on the next project in that area' rather than 'I could tell they had improved'
must_avoid: describing the teammate's promotion as the evidence of growth — promotions are organizational decisions; the capability artifact should be named separately

availability-based mentoring ('I was always there when they needed me') — being present is not developing a skill; the story requires the candidate to have designed the growth path, not just been accessible
vague 'they grew a lot' or 'they're great now' framing — these are the candidate's impressions, not signals of the teammate's capability

centering the candidate's effort rather than the teammate's outcome — if the story is about how much the candidate invested, it is an Excellence story about effort; if it's about what the teammate can now do, it is ST
